title: The Impact of Restricting Labor Mobility on Corporate Investment and Entrepreneurship
abstract: This paper examines how labor mobility restrictions like noncompete agreements affect firms’ investment decisions. Using matched employee-employer data from LinkedIn, I show that increases in the enforceability of noncompete agreements lead to widespread declines in employee departures, specifically in knowledge-intensive occupations. Established firms that rely more on these knowledge-intensive occupations increase their investment rate in physical capital. However, new firm entry in corresponding sectors declines. I provide evidence for different mechanisms to explain these patterns. Together, the findings show that labor frictions play an important role in investment decisions.

article.1 Institutional Background:
JEL E22 - Investment; Capital; Intangible Capital; Capacity J24 - Human Capital; Skills; Occupational Choice; Labor Productivity J41 - Labor Contracts J62 - Job, Occupational, and Intergenerational Mobility K31 - Labor Law L26 - Entrepreneurship Issue Section: Article Editor: Manju Puri Manju Puri Editor Search for other works by this author on: Oxford Academic Google Scholar

Recent research and policy proposals have renewed the debate over agreements that restrict workers from leaving their employers. In January 2023, the Federal Trade Commission (FTC) proposed a rule to ban noncompete agreements (NCs), a specific type of contract that precludes employees from moving to a competitor after leaving their employer.1Proponents of the ban point to studies indicating mobility restrictions can dampen innovation and regional growth (Saxenian 1994;Gilson 1999;Marx, Strumsky, and Fleming 2009), and hurt workers (Johnson, Lavetti, and Lipsitz 2021;Starr, Prescott, and Bishara 2021). Opponents argue these restrictive agreements are an important way to protect and encourage firms’ investment (Barnet and Sichelman 2020). This paper evaluates the latter claim by measuring the impact of NCs on investment rates, both at existing firms and in the form of new firm entry.

Ex ante, the effect of NCs on investment is not obvious. In a Coasian world with perfect labor markets and no informational frictions, wages should adjust to compensate workers for the restrictions, leaving employment and firms’ optimal policies unchanged (Coase 1960). In practice, several layers of frictions may cause firms to adjust their investment rate when labor conditions change, and the direction is ambiguous (Bai, Fairhurst, and Serfling 2020). In the case of stronger labor mobility restrictions, if wages do not fully adjust, firms may decrease their investment rate as they shift to relatively cheaper labor. NCs could also depress workers’ incentives or productivity (Garmaise 2011;Fulghieri and Sevilir 2011), reducing the return to investment. On the other hand, complementarity between skilled labor and capital may lead firms to increase their investment rate as they accumulate more human capital through greater worker retention. NCs can also help firms make investments by reducing the possibility of an ex post holdup by workers (Acemoglu and Shimer 1999;Conti 2014). Finally, if mobility restrictions slow the entry of new firms, incumbent firms may also benefit from a reduction in competitive pressure and increase investment as a result of lower constraints (Kang and Fleming 2020).

I exploit changes in the enforceability of NCs to test the impact of labor mobility restrictions on investment rate and on firm entry. These changes come mainly from state-level court rulings that overturn precedent and cause a sudden change in the enforceability of NC contracts. Besides policy relevance, NCs provide a useful setting to test mobility restrictions because they are commonly used, including for rank and file employees. I introduce detailed employer-employee matched histories from LinkedIn to verify these changes in NC enforceability affect labor mobility in my setting, and to understand which moves are most affected.2After matching to public companies with investment data, my sample covers 5 to 6 million individuals each year, across all levels of seniority, occupation types, and states across the US. Later, I also use LinkedIn company pages (including of defunct firms) to study the entry of new firms in the economy.

I find that increased NC enforceability leads departure rates to decline by 7% to 11% for workers in knowledge-intensive occupations, defined as occupations where a majority of workers have a bachelor’s degree or higher. In my setting there is no statistically significant effect on mobility in other occupations. This pattern is consistent with work showing NCs primarily affect college-educated and skilled workers (Johnson, Lavetti, and Lipsitz 2021;Starr, Prescott, and Bishara 2021). As one would expect for NCs, the greatest declines occur in moves to the same occupation type or industry sector.

I then show that these declines in labor mobility lead to a higher investment rate in physical capital. The increase in investment occurs specifically in firms that rely more on knowledge-intensive occupations. The results support the idea that NCs help retain human capital that is complementary to physical capital, as inAutor, Levy, and Murnane (2003). Additionally, more enforceable NCs reduce employees’ ability to extract rents from the firm after costly investments have been made, which can also encourage more investment.

To further understand the mechanisms behind the increase in investment, I devise additional cross-sectional tests. First, firms with higher labor-to-capital ratios may be more susceptible to holdup by workers, all else equal.3I show that the relationship between investment and NC enforceability is indeed stronger in these firms. Second, the ability to retain existing workers should help firms to increase investment in their core business, as opposed to secondary areas which would more likely involve hiring new workers. I show that for firms operating in multiple segments, increases in physical investment go towards the main sector of operation.

Third, I split firms by the ex ante level of competition they face. Competitive pressure can lead to lower investment by diminishing the firm’s return on investment (Gilbert 2006) or inducing financial constraints (Fazzari, Hubbard, and Petersen 1988), and NCs may reduce competition for incumbent firms (Kang and Fleming 2020). Stronger NCs could therefore lead to higher investment rates by alleviating competitive pressures, independent of the effect on the firm’s own workers. In line with this, I find that in the highest competition tercile of firms, increased NC enforceability leads to higher investment rates at all firms, not just at knowledge-intensive firms.

Lastly, I explore the effect of NCs on other forms of investment. Recent work on investment theory has rightly emphasized the increasingly important role of intangible investments (Peters and Taylor 2017;Crouzet and Eberly 2023). However, empirical work finds that intangible investment responds in the opposite way from physical investment when it comes to other forms of labor frictions (Bai, Fairhurst, and Serfling 2020;Acharya, Baghai, and Subramanian 2014). In my setting, I find no statistically significant change in R&D investment rate or patent applications following increased NC enforceability. This may be explained by NCs adversely affecting employee incentives (Garmaise 2011), and employee effort being an especially important input for innovation (Fulghieri and Sevilir 2011).

Similarly, investment in new firms may also work differently than investment in existing firms. In addition to the motivation effects mentioned above, new firms are especially responsive to local investment opportunities (Adelino, Ma, and Robinson 2017) and to local labor availability (Nimier-David 2022). It is therefore unclear whether we should expect NCs to affect new firms in the same way as existing firms. To examine this question, I use firm entry as a proxy for the extensive margin decision to invest in a new firm. I find that even as NCs lead to higher physical investment at existing knowledge-intensive firms, new firm entry declines in corresponding sectors. Taken together, the results suggest that restrictions on workers play an important role in whether new projects are started within incumbent firms or new firms, that is, where the boundaries of the firm are drawn (Coase 1937).

The results stand up to a range of robustness analyses. Throughout, specifications include industry-year fixed effects to account for time-varying industry conditions and firm fixed effects to allow for different firm-specific baselines. Results are robust to including state- and firm-level controls, and even state-year fixed effects, which account nonparametrically for potential time-varying local conditions. Restricting firms to be more geographically concentrated, so that a greater fraction of employees are in the treated state, makes results stronger. I also show that the sample composition is stable over time, including in the share of workers in knowledge-intensive occupations. Additionally, a placebo test in control states shows zero differential trend for departure rates in knowledge-intensive occupations compared to other occupations, when NC enforceability does not change.

This paper builds on a rich labor mobility literature studying the effect of NCs both on specific sets of individuals (e.g. inventors [Marx, Strumsky, and Fleming 2009], CEOs [Garmaise 2011] and new ventures (e.g. medical practices [Hausman and Lavetti 2021], banks [Bird and Knopf 2015]).4This paper extends the results to millions of employment histories and adds cross-sectional results, showing that the decrease in departures is driven by workers in a range of knowledge-intensive occupations, and that new firm entry declines across knowledge-intensive sectors following a change in NC enforceability. In doing so, it helps to establish that the impact of NCs is both deep and widespread, countering criticism that the impact of NCs is narrow and likely overestimated (Barnet and Sichelman 2020).

This paper also relates to questions raised byZingales (2000)about corporate finance in the context of firms’ increasing dependence on human capital.Garmaise (2011)considers the impact of NCs on CEOs’ human capital investment and the skill-intensity of production, and finds that these decline when NCs are stronger. This paper complementsGarmaise (2011)by looking at firms’ investment rates by unit of capital rather than unit of labor, to better align with most theories of investment (Bai, Fairhurst, and Serfling 2020), and by separating out the effect for different cross-sections of firms.

In this sense, the paper is closer to those ofAutor, Kerr, and Kugler (2007)andBai, Fairhurst, and Serfling (2020), who study the impact of employment protections on firms’ investment policy. FollowingBai, Fairhurst, and Serfling (2020), I focus on investment rate rather than level as the more relevant measure flowing from the q-theory model of investment. Employment protections also induce lower turnover, but by making it more difficult for firms to lay off undesired workers; instead, NCs make it easier for firms to retain desired workers. This difference is critical: while employment protections decrease firm investment rates, I find NCs increase them.

Finally, this paper contributes to our understanding of business dynamism by investigating a factor that impedes human capital reallocation. Firm entry has declined in recent decades, arguably because of a decline in allocative efficiency that has slowed productivity growth (Decker et al. 2014,2017;Kang and Fleming 2020). This paper shows NCs may contribute to slowing business dynamism by increasing existing firms’ investment rates while depressing new firm entry.
This section lays out relevant institutional details for the empirical setting, including the prevalence of NCs and the enforcement changes examined in this paper. Further institutional details are provided inAppendix I.B.

1.1 Prevalence and enforceability of NCs

NCs are a useful setting to study labor mobility because of their relatively widespread use. In a survey of about 11,000 U.S. labor force participants,Starr, Prescott, and Bishara (2021)find that 38% of workers have at some time signed a NC, and that 18% are subject to one at the time of the survey. NCs are more common for more knowledge-intensive positions:Starr, Prescott, and Bishara (2021)estimate that 39% of workers with professional degrees have NCs, and 35% of workers in architecture, engineering or computer and mathematical occupations are subject to a NC.Table A.1contains information fromStarr, Prescott, and Bishara (2021)on NC incidence by occupation, alongside educational attainment statistics from the Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS). NCs are more common in occupations where a majority of employees have completed a bachelor’s degree, but they are still present in less “skilled” positions. Anecdotally, news organizations from theWall Street Journalto theWashington Postand theAtlantichave reported on the general increased prevalence of NCs over the past 15 years.5

NCs are governed at the state level, like most employment law. There is wide variation across states in the type of NCs that are permitted and how they are enforced. At one extreme, California and North Dakota have banned the use of NCs; at the other extreme, Florida allows enforcement of NCs even for employees who are laid off.Bishara (2011)identifies six broad dimensions of state NC enforcement, which together determine the strength of NC enforceability in a particular state. I review these dimensions of enforceability in detail inAppendix I.B.

The main reason that NCs affect mobility is deterrence: employees avoid moving to a new job because they fear the consequences of breaching their contracts. After an increase in NC enforceability, employees who seek legal advice about a potential move are more likely to receive advice that discourages them from leaving their employer. For example, following Texas’ decision inMarsh v. Cook(2011), attorneys counseled employees that the risk of litigation for a NC breach had increased.6Employers also play a role in transmitting the effect of more enforceable NCs. Firms can and do remind workers of their obligations when a worker expresses interest in leaving.7Finally, in states that allow modification, employers have incentives to draft NCs that bar workers from a broader set of moves (Bishara, Martin, and Thomas 2015).

Nevertheless, some factors can lessen the impact of NCs. For instance, employers may selectively apply and enforce NCs for a specific group of employees, such as top executives or those with access to trade secrets (Barnet and Sichelman 2020). NC-bound employees who wish to leave could also bargain with their employers, and reach a mutually beneficial agreement that still allows them to leave. In these cases, we may not see meaningful changes in departure rates among rank-and-file employees. Section 5.1 verifies that NC enforceability negatively affects labor mobility, driven by workers in knowledge-intensive occupations.

1.2 Empirical setting: Changes in NC enforceability

I look for discrete, plausibly exogenous changes to NC enforceability to see how varying labor mobility restrictions affect investment and new firm entry. NCs are governed by both statute and precedent, meaning that NC enforcement can change at the state level two ways: either the legislature changes the statute or the state’s high court makes a decision that sets a new precedent that lower courts must follow.Table 1outlines the NC enforcement changes that I use for my empirical setting: seven state Supreme Court rulings, one Appellate Court ruling, and one change from the state legislature, occurring from 2009 to 2013.8If the change took place in the last 3 months of the calendar year, I assign the following year as the relevant first year of change.

Since Illinois first expands the scope of NCs then reduces their enforceability, I code Illinois as an increase in 2012 but no longer an increase starting in 2013. I first identify court rulings by combing through practitioner blogs and verify all cases using Westlaw, a leading provider of data for legal professionals. I focus on rulings from states’ highest courts because these represent the “final word” for state law and apply to the entire state. Court decisions are retroactive, meaning that they affect the enforceability of all NCs, including NCs entered into before the decision was issued.

To intuitively check the unexpectedness and importance of each court decision, I search for what local attorneys wrote about each decision at the time and confirm the expected impact is meaningful. InAppendix I.B, I further investigate whether judges faced upcoming reelections. Only three of the states feature contested elections for judges, two feature uncontested retention elections and two use appointment by the general assembly. On average, judges were 6 to 7 years from reelection or reappointment at the time of their decisions, making it unlikely that decisions were affected by immediate political concerns.

Two court decisions in my sample merit special notes. In Illinois, the state supreme court’s decision to expand the scope of business interests inReliable Fire Equipment v. Arredondo et al.was followed the next year by an appellate court decision, which the state supreme court declined to revisit, that decreased the enforceability of NCs. To reflect this, I code Illinois as an increase in 2012 but no longer an increase in 2013.9In Montana, the decision concerns the applicability of NCs to terminated employees and is therefore a more narrow change. I verify that my results are robust to excluding Montana inAppendix I.A.

Second, in addition to these court rulings, I note that Georgia passed a law making NCs more enforceable in 2011. With law changes, we may be more concerned about anticipation or lobbying than court decisions. In Georgia’s case, there was in fact substantial uncertainty leading up to the change (Ewens and Marx 2017).10The statute change was not retroactive: employers had to secure new NCs with their employees in order to benefit from modification. Since the Georgia change occurs during my sample period, I include it in my main specifications, but also verify inAppendix I.Athat my results are robust to removing the state.

The “treated” states constitute a geographically and economically diverse group of states. In Section 4 andAppendix I.A, I show that these states are similar to the control states in terms of political and economic characteristics, as well as levels of NC enforceability in 2009. I also show that treated and control states follow similar trends in terms of macroeconomic conditions such GDP per capita, GDP growth, population growth and unemployment rate. This suggests the changes in enforceability are not simply a response to a relatively better or worse environment in the treated states.
article.2 Theoretical Motivation:
Ex ante, the relationship between labor mobility and investment policy is not obvious. To start with, NCs can be viewed as a transfer from employees to employers: under a NC, an employee provides a guarantee to her employer that she will not compete for a period of time after leaving, which is valuable to the employer and costly to herself. In a Coasian world with no informational frictions, wages should adjust to compensate workers for the restrictions, leaving employment and firms’ optimal policies unchanged (Coase 1960).

In practice, evidence indicates wages fall rather than increase when NCs are stronger (Johnson, Lavetti, and Lipsitz 2021;Starr, Prescott, and Bishara 2021), suggesting that firms’ optimal policies may also change in response to NCs. Moreover, uncertainty and irreversibility on both the firms’ and the workers’ sides may create holdup problems that are affected by NCs. The nature of these holdup problems depends on the type of investment, the firm’s reliance on skilled labor, and other industry conditions, such as the level of competition.

In the sections below, I discuss the possible effects on physical investment (e.g., capital expenditures), then on intangible investment (e.g., R&D). I also review the possible effects on entrepreneurship. I discuss the results in the context of other work on employment restrictions in Appendix I.D.

2.1 Physical investment

The literature is indeterminate on the effect of mobility restrictions like NCs on physical capital investment. There are at least four forces to take into consideration, including whether labor and capital are complements, the potential holdup that workers exercise on the firm, workers’ incentives, and competition.

First, the effect of NCs on physical investment depends in part on whether physical and human capital are complements or substitutes. If they are substitutes and NCs cause labor to become relatively cheaper, this could lead firms to shift away from capital-intensive assets in favor of relatively cheaper labor-intensive assets, causing lower investment rates in physical capital (Caballero and Hammour 1998). However, if physical and human capital are complements, NCs could lead firms to increase physical investment in order to match the higher accumulation rate of human capital caused by NCs.11

Autor, Levy, and Murnane (2003)show that physical capital tends to complement workers performing nonroutine problem-solving and complex communication tasks, tasks which often show up in “skilled” occupations. Indeed,Larrain (2014)shows that when it is easier for firms to accumulate physical capital, there is higher demand for skilled labor. A symmetric relationship may hold with NCs, where easier accumulation of skilled labor leads to higher demand for physical capital. In this case, investment rates should increase especially in firms with a greater fraction of skilled workers (“knowledge-intensive firms”).

Hypothesis 1 (Complementarity of Capital). When human and physical capital are substitutes, NCs lead firms to shift away from capital-intensive assets and reduce investment in physical capital. When physical and human capital are complements (e.g., in knowledge-intensive firms), NCs lead firms to increase investment in physical capital.

A second consideration that may influence how NCs affect physical investment is the holdup problem that workers exercise on the firm. Workers’ ability to extract rents from the firm after an investment is made, whether by negotiating higher wages (Acemoglu and Shimer 1999) or transferring knowledge to a competitor (Conti 2014), reduces the firm’s ex ante incentive to invest. Stronger NCs reduce employees’ ability to extract rents by threatening to leave. This alleviates the holdup problem and can induce higher investment rates. Since NCs occur more frequently among knowledge-intensive occupations, and knowledge transfer is a greater threat for these occupations, the effect should manifest most for knowledge-intensive firms.

Hypothesis 2 (Holdup Problem). In the presence of a holdup problem (of workers over firms), an increase in NC enforceability leads to higher physical investment rates, especially for knowledge-intensive firms.

A third channel moderates NCs’ impact on physical investment: workers’ incentives.Belot, Boone, and Van Ours (2007)argue that employment protections can encourage firms to invest in firm-specific skills and enhance productivity. Conversely, provisions that decrease employees’ bargaining power, like NCs, could decrease employee incentives to invest in firm-specific skills and reduce their productivity.Garmaise (2011)uses a similar argument to explain why stronger NCs could lower physical investment: in his framework, NCs lower managers’ investment in their general human capital and lead firms to undertake less skill-intensive production, on a per-employee basis. In both of these situations, the total effect on investment rate will depend on the extent to which increased retention offsets any declines in employee-level productivity, as well as on the complementarity of physical and human capital.

Hypothesis 3 (Employee Incentives). If NCs reduce worker productivity more than they increase firm-level human capital, and physical and human capital are complements (e.g., in knowledge-intensive firms), then increases in NC enforceability decrease investment in physical capital. Conversely, if NCs reduce worker productivity less than they increase firm-level human capital, and physical and human capital are complements, then increases in NC enforceability increase investment in physical capital.

Lastly, NCs’ effects on investment may vary with the competitive environment.Kang and Fleming (2020)argue that NCs reduce competition by making it difficult for potential new competitors to hire workers. (Indeed, Section 5.4 shows that increases in NC enforceability lead to lower firm entry and fewer moves to new firms in affected occupations.) Lower competition can improve returns to investment and reduce financial constraints, making investment more attractive (Gilbert 2006;Fazzari, Hubbard, and Petersen 1988;Grieser and Liu 2019). Notably, this channel does not depend on the mobility of the firm’s own workers. Instead, it depends on the ex ante level of competition facing existing firms and the extent to which NCs reduce competition in their product markets.

This channel is most relevant when firms face high ex ante competitive constraints. Where competition is high, incumbent firms should benefit from a reduction in competition, even if their own workers are not directly targeted. When firms face ex ante low competition, this channel is less relevant. The additional slack afforded by reduced competition should matter much less, and other mechanisms driving higher investment in knowledge-intensive firms (e.g., H1 or H2) should dominate.

Hypothesis 4 (Competition). In markets with high competition (ex ante), increases in NC enforceability alleviate competitive pressure and increase investment in physical capital across all firms. In markets with already low competition, reduced competition is not a first-order concern, and the previous hypotheses hold.

2.2 Intangible investment, R&D, and innovation

Recent work on investment theory has rightly emphasized the increasingly important role of intangible investments (Peters and Taylor 2017;Crouzet and Eberly 2023). At the macro level, papers likePeters and Taylor (2017)treat intangible investment (a combination of R&D and “organizational capital investment” measured by SG&A) in much the same way as tangible investment, though they do note that intangible capital is more costly to adjust and adjusts more slowly to changes in investment opportunities.

Empirical work looking at labor frictions, however, has drawn different conclusions.Bai, Fairhurst, and Serfling (2020)find that R&D increases with employment protection laws (EPLs), the opposite of what happens to the physical investment rate; this finding is consistent with that ofAcharya, Baghai, and Subramanian (2014), who find that innovation increases with EPLs. The logic is that EPLs reduce thefirm’sholdup over employees, which increases employee incentives to innovate, and (implicitly) that this channel dominates any firm disincentives to invest in innovation. Following this logic, if NCs decrease employee incentives by increasing the potential holdup of firms over workers, then we should see less innovation after NCs become more enforceable (Acharya, Baghai, and Subramanian 2014;Fulghieri and Sevilir 2011).

The different predictions for tangible and intangible investments stem from the relative importance of employee incentives in producing innovation. In the case of innovation, bothAcharya, Baghai, and Subramanian (2014)andFulghieri and Sevilir (2011)emphasize the central role of employee discretion in producing innovation. Using the framework from H3, the employee-level effect may dominate when it comes to producing innovation or making investments specific to the production of innovation, like R&D. The firm-level effect may dominate when it comes to the production of physical outputs or making tangible investments to support the production of those physical outputs.

Hypothesis 5 (Intangible Investments). If NCs reduce workers’ innovation productivity more than they increase firm-level human capital, then increases in NC enforceability decrease investment in intangible capital. Conversely, if NCs reduce workers’ innovation productivity less than they increase firm-level human capital, then increases in NC enforceability increase investment in intangible capital.

Outside of the theory, it is worth noting that intangible investment has specific measurement challenges. The majority of R&D expenses are wages (Hall and Lerner 2010), and the possibility that wages change in response to labor frictions (Starr, Prescott, and Bishara 2021;Johnson, Lavetti, and Lipsitz 2021) makes it difficult to disentangle the quantity of intangible investment from the price of human capital. Recent measures of intangible investment, like those fromPeters and Taylor (2017), still largely rely on R&D. Patents and patent citations, meanwhile, have their own limitations in measuring innovation for two reasons: not all firms use patents to protect innovation, and patent citations are tricky to forecast for recent innovations. Additional caution therefore applies in interpreting the results of these analyses.

2.3 Investment in new firms: Entrepreneurship

Another distinction that warrants discussion is investment in new firms, which may have different sensitivities than investment in existing firms. Multiple studies indicate that young firms are more sensitive to financial constraints when it comes to investment (Gonzales-Uribe and Paravisini 2019;Ma, Murfin, and Pratt 2022).Adelino, Ma, and Robinson (2017)also show that new firms are more responsive to local investment opportunities. In addition, new firms are highly sensitive to the availability of human capital (Nimier-David 2022). They also may stand to benefit the most from investment protections (Barnet and Sichelman 2020). It is therefore unclear whether we should expect NCs to affect new firms in the same way as existing firms.

The question of how NCs affect entrepreneurial activity is not a new one, and in fact has long been a subject of debate in both academic and policy circles (Saxenian 1994;Gilson 1999;Fallick, Fleischman, and Rebitzer 2006;Starr, Balasubramanian, and Sakakibara 2018;Barnet and Sichelman 2020;White House Report 2016). Given the importance of new firms for economic growth and innovation (Decker et al. 2017), I review the theory relevant to this special case below. Since it is difficult to distinguish discrete investment decisions at new firms from the decision to launch a business, I generally refer to firm entry below as the principal new firm investment decision.

On the positive side, an increased ability to retain human capital through NCs could benefit new firms by reducing turnover, especially in knowledge-intensive sectors (H1). New firms could also benefit from a reduction in holdup problems, again especially in knowledge-intensive sectors (H2). If these forces dominate, then more enforceable NCs could in fact lead to an increase in new firm investment and entry.

On the negative side, at least three forces combine to make NCs potentially more costly for new firms. First, while NCs may emhance new firms’ ability to retain human capital, they may also hinder the acquisition of suitable talent in the first place. Several models highlight the role of NCs in preventing potential founders from launching their projects (Franco and Mitchell 2008;Fulghieri and Sevilir 2011). Additionally, new firms are particularly sensitive to labor availability for new hires in early stages (Nimier-David 2022), and they typically have less bargaining power in the hiring process compared to established firms (Babina et al. 2019). Therefore, from a labor acquisition perspective, NCs are likely to impose additional costs on new firms and dampen their entry, particularly in sectors that rely more on occupations bound by NCs.

Second, similar to innovation, investment in new firms requires a high level of employee discretion and motivation (Fulghieri and Sevilir 2011). Moreover, since new firms are typically small, a decline in worker-level productivity may be more difficult to offset through an increase in firm-level human capital. Both attributes increase the likelihood of new firm investment behaving as in the first part of H3 and H5, that is, with an increase in NC enforceability causing a decrease in firm entry.

Third,Adelino, Ma, and Robinson (2017)show that new firms are sensitive to investment opportunities. If incumbent firms engage in more investment, that additional activity may crowd out new firm entry. This scenario is similar to the one described inSaxenian (1994), who argues that NCs lead to a slowing down of creative destruction.

The factors described above are not mutually exclusive. For example, the displacement of firm entry by incumbent activity may be exacerbated by difficulties with labor acquisition and incentives. Alternatively, the benefit of increased investment protection could compensate for diminished investment opportunities. Whether NC enforceability increases or decreases aggregate firm entry depends on the net effect of these combined factors.

Hypothesis 6 (Firm Entry). If concerns about retention and holdup problems dominate for new firms, then increases in NC enforceability increase firm entry. If instead issues of labor availability, incentives, or investment opportunities dominate for new firms, then increases in NC enforceability decrease firm entry.

Different forces may dominate in different sectors. A corollary of H6 is that if NCs decrease the availability of some occupations more than others, or reduce investment opportunities in some sectors more than others, entry is relatively more likely to decrease for new firms that rely on these occupations or belong in these sectors.
article.3 Data:
This section lays out the paper’s approach for taking Section 2’s questions to the data. I first describe the LinkedIn data, with a focus on how these data are used to capture knowledge-intensive occupations, departure rates, and new firm entry, as well as discussing potential selection issues and coverage. I then describe the data used for measuring investments and sample construction for the main analyses.

3.1 LinkedIn data

LinkedIn is an online professional networking platform, which began in 2003 and has since grown to over 900 million users worldwide. In 2015, LinkedIn selected a small number of researchers to participate in its Economic Graph Challenge. Awardees were granted access to detailed deidentified data from LinkedIn’s platform. The data contain no name information and numerical member identifiers in the data are hashed.

LinkedIn data provides a unique opportunity to study the effects of NCs because of the depth and breadth of coverage. Users’ profiles on LinkedIn are essentially online CVs, listing the history of where they have worked and in what capacity. LinkedIn standardizes this information, both at the position level (e.g., occupation categories) and at the employer level (e.g., mapping employers to unique pages which include the business’s industry, year founded, size, and ownership type). This makes it possible to test predictions for different types of firms and workers.

I use the start years and end years from the deidentified employment histories, along with information on the employers, to create a panel of employee movements from and to firms. Since pages function as CVs, individuals have incentive to post complete employment histories, which means the data are backfilled. For example, an individual could become a LinkedIn member in 2015, but if her employment history started in 2005 she would be an observation in my panel starting in 2005. This feature mitigates selection issues based on when individuals create LinkedIn profiles.

An equally important feature of the data is the tendency of LinkedIn users to keep their profiles up to date, driven by the public nature of the profiles. In spot checks, the vast majority of profiles are found to be accurate within a calendar year. For my analysis, I use data from a recent (2021) snapshot of employment histories, so that I observe profiles through 2018 with at least a 3-year lag. If individuals do not log into LinkedIn for that long, they are tagged as inactive accounts and I drop the entire employment history from my sample. Later in this section, I present a few tests showing that cross-sectional properties of the data (e.g., share of knowledge-intensive occupations, geographic concentration) are stable over time, again alleviating concerns that the selection of individuals changes over time.

I match firms in LinkedIn to publicly held firms in the Compustat database in order to obtain additional firm characteristics, such as firm investment, total assets, and NAICS industry. The main sample of analysis in the paper consists of these matched public firms. The exception is the analysis of firm entry, which covers the entry of nonpublic firms, as explained in Section 3.1.3. Comparing the number of employees reported in Compustat to the number of LinkedIn employee profiles for the same set of matched public firms (Table 2, panels A and C), LinkedIn contains on average profiles for 30% (median 40%) of the firms’ employees.

3.1.1 Knowledge workers and firms

As outlined in Section 2, knowledge-intensive occupations are of particular interest for several reasons. They complement physical capital (Autor, Levy, and Murnane 2003), involve a holdup problem related to knowledge leakage (Conti 2014), and are more likely to be bound by NCs (Starr, Prescott, and Bishara 2021).

I define knowledge-intensive occupations using educational attainment statistics from the BLS.Table A.1reports the percentage of workers in each occupation holding a bachelor’s degree or higher, and the average years of education (high school and above). It also reports numbers fromStarr, Prescott, and Bishara (2021)on NC incidence by occupation. There is a clear break in educational attainment between occupations from “Life, Physical, Social Sciences” to “Management Occupations,” on the one hand, and occupations from “Sales and Related Occupations” to “Construction and Extraction Occupations” on the other. I group the former into “knowledge workers,” reflecting that a majority of workers in these occupations hold a Bachelor’s degree or higher.12There are two exceptions. First, LinkedIn’s classification scheme provides one category for healthcare services, so “Healthcare Support” occupations are grouped with “Healthcare Practitioners and Technical Occupations” and considered knowledge workers. Second, the American Bar Association holds that NCs applied to lawyers are unethical, and its Rule of Professional Conduct prohibits lawyers from creating an agreement that “restricts the right of a lawyer to practice after termination of the relationship” (Horvitz 2016). Consequently, I drop legal occupations from my analysis. InAppendix I.A, I run a placebo test on legal occupations and show the effect on their departure rates is indistinguishable from zero, economically and statistically.

Knowledge workers represent about half of the workers for the average firm in my sample. This is higher than the implied equivalent fraction in the BLS: numbers fromTable A.1indicate approximately 33% of knowledge workers in the total economy. Generally, oversampling of one group might represent a concern for external or internal validity. However, I argue this is not the case here. In terms of external validity, knowledge-intensive sectors are precisely the population of most interest in this paper (Section 2). In terms of internal validity, I include industry-year and firm fixed effects throughout my empirical analysis to absorb any baseline differences that may exist across industries. The estimates represent within-firm, within-industry-year relative differences in outcome variables. Section 4 discusses this in detail. InAppendix I.A, I also show that the fraction of knowledge workers in firms is very stable over time. In addition, I run a placebo test showing there are no differential trends in departure rates between knowledge workers and other workers in control states.

Knowledgefirmsare firms which employ a greater than median fraction of knowledge workers. I use this classification to proxy for firms that are more dependent on knowledge-intensive occupations, as described in the hypotheses from Section 2. This designation varies at the firm level rather than the industry level, allowing for within-industry comparison. For example, 47% of firms in NAICS group 32 (manufacturing - organic and chemical) and 52% of firms in NAICS group 33 (manufacturing - metal and electronic) are knowledge firms.

Finally, I define a knowledgesectorfor new firm entry using the industry designation given by LinkedIn. This knowledge sector includes industries listed under either the Technology or Professional, scientific & technical services sectors. I choose this grouping because it most directly maps to the knowledge occupations described above.

3.1.2 Departure rates

An individual counts as employed at company A in yeartif she lists a job at company A at any time during yeart. She is considered as a departure in yeartif she is no longer employed at company A in yeart+ 1. Importantly, this applies even if she moves out of state, and even if she does not list a new position in the next year. The total number of employees is the number of distinct individuals listed on LinkedIn as working at the firm in that year. The total departure rate of a firm is its number of departures scaled by the number of employees, as long as occupation is known and that occupation is not in the legal domain. I divide by the total number of employees throughout to make sure results are driven by the numerator, and not by a changing denominator, and also to make the interpretation of triple differences estimations more straightforward.

Table 2, panel A contains summary statistics for departure rates. The total average departure rate is 13.2% (median 11.9%), and the average knowledge worker departure rate scaled by total size is 6.9% (median 5.8%). In other words, knowledge workers represent a little under half of all departures. An average departure rate of 13.2% implies an average tenure length of 7.6 years if departures are a Poisson process. In comparison, the Current Population Survey supplements place average tenure between 2004 and 2018 at 8.12 years.13

Additionally, the richness of LinkedIn’s data allows me to consider specific departure types, such as moves within occupation or industry. “Same occupation” moves are departures where the new position is in the same occupation category as the old position (e.g., engineering to engineering), while “different occupation” moves capture those where there is a change in the occupation category (e.g., engineering to finance). Likewise, “same sector” moves are the subset of departures where the new position is in the same industry sector as the old position, while “different sector” moves capture instances where the new position is in a different industry sector than the old position. All variables are scaled by number of employees, so the total departure rate is mechanically higher than all other subset departure rates.

3.1.3 Entrepreneurial activity

I also use LinkedIn company pages to measure the entry of new firms. Using year founded, location, and industry, I create an industry-state panel of new firms entering the economy. I limit the data to private companies and exclude financial firms. Company pages do not have NAICS codes. Instead, an industry classification within LinkedIn contains roughly 150 unique industries. To make the numbers comparable across states, I scale the number of newly founded firms by population. InTable 2, panel B, I show summary statistics for both scaled and unscaled observations for easier interpretation. The unit of observation is state-year-industry. The average number of firms entering per year per industry per state is 6.3, while the median is one. When solo firms (firms classified as “myself only” in LinkedIn) are excluded, the average drops to 5.5. The rate is also 5.5 for new firms with fewer than 50 employees.

These data are the best way to capture new firm entry for my analysis for several reasons. First, I require a panel of new firm entry between 2004 and 2018 with state, industry, and year information. This is surprisingly difficult to obtain: many data sets either end too early or do not have both state and industry information. Second, to align with the theoretical motivation in Section 2 and the rest of the paper’s empirical analysis, I need to map new firms to knowledge-intensive sectors or occupations. Using LinkedIn for this mapping is the most natural, as the definitions are internally consistent. Third, my predictions concern new firms, so it is important that I am able to capture the creation of firms rather than establishments.

As a validation check for the quality of the firm entry data that I use, I obtain data from VentureXpert, a database of private equity and venture capital deals from which new VC-backed firm entry can be constructed. I select a random sample of 40 firms from VentureXpert, equally split between ventures listed as now-defunct and ventures listed as still-active (including acquired). I find matches for 36 (90%) of these ventures in the LinkedIn data, equally distributed among the defunct and not defunct. Additionally, the number of new firm entries in LinkedIn is two orders of magnitude greater than the equivalent number in VentureXpert, and less heavily skewed toward California.

3.2 Investment data and sample construction

I use Compustat data to measure the investment response of firms. I define net investment as capital expenditures minus the sale of property (capxv - sppe) and scale by 1-year-lagged total assets (at) to obtain the physical investment rate. Results are robust to scaling by gross or net capital instead (ppegt or ppent, seeAppendix I.A).

To measure intangible investments, I use two proxies, neither of which is perfect but together shed light on other forms of investment. The first measure most closely echoes the physical investment rate, and corresponds to research and development expenses (xrd) scaled by 1-year-lagged total assets (at). This is an imperfect measure because a large share of R&D consists of wages paid to research activities (Hall and Lerner 2010), and wages have their own dynamic in this context (Johnson, Lavetti, and Lipsitz 2021;Starr 2019). Moreover, not all firms report R&D expenses; in the results here, I do not replace missing R&D with zeros, but results are very similar if missing entries are set to zero instead. The second measure is a log transformation of one plus the number of patent applications in a given year.14This is closer to an output measure since intangible investment does not always result in patent applications, but has also widely been used in the literature as a proxy for innovative activity (e.g.,Acharya, Baghai, and Subramanian 2014).

In constructing the sample, I exclude firm-year observations with more than 100% growth in sales or assets to avoid bias from mergers or acquisitions. I also remove financial and regulated industries and exclude observations with missing stock market data (firms that are not yet public). I drop observations with less than 1 million in assets, and winsorize variables at 1%. These steps are to ensure that my sample is focused on the most relevant observations and that my estimates are not driven by outliers, but my results are robust to less stringent cleaning as well.

Table 2, panel C, presents summary statistics for this sample. The average investment rate is 0.04, and the median 0.02. Knowledge firms – firms employing a higher than median fraction of knowledge workers – have similar investment rates, and tend to be slightly smaller.
article.4 Methodology:
The variation I exploit to test my hypotheses comes from staggered state-level changes in the enforceability of NCs. This lends itself to a difference-in-differences approach, comparing the change in outcomes for treated states with the change in outcomes for control states over the same period.

Recent work in econometrics has highlighted potential issues that can arise with heterogeneous treatment in difference-in-differences, in particular with staggered treatment when pre and post windows are of different lengths (Goodman-Bacon 2021;De Chaisemartin and d’Haultfoeuille 2020). To address these issues, I employ a “stacked” difference-in-differences approach as inGormley and Matsa (2011)andCengiz et al. (2019). This approach is robust to heterogeneity across groups and periods when treatment adoption is staggered (Gardner 2021).

The intuition underlying the stacked approach is to form distinct cohorts for each treatment year, capturing a fixed time window before and after the treatment. These cohorts are then “stacked” together to form a panel that aligns the data in relation to the treatment timing, rather than in calendar time.Gardner (2021)shows that this approach is equivalent to estimating an average treatment effect in each cohort and then taking the average of the cohort-specific estimators, weighted by the relative sizes of the cohorts. In this way, the approach is similar to the idea inSun and Abraham (2021)andCallaway and Sant’Anna (2021)to estimate separate average treatment effects in different groups and then aggregate these estimators to form an overall estimate of the treatment effect.

For each treatment year listed inTable 1, Icreate a cohortcof treated and control observations in a 10-year window around the year of treatment (5 years before and 5 years after). The first cohort is for Wisconsin’s treatment in 2009, and includes calendar years 2004 through 2014. The last cohort is for Virginia’s treatment in 2013, and includes calendar years 2008 through 2018. Controls are states that are never in the treated group.

The baseline specification is as follows, for companyiin industryj, states, yeartand cohortc:

α β γ θ ϵ y ijstc = α + β ( treate d s * pos t t ) + γ i c + θ jtc + ϵ ijstc , (1)

wheretreated*postis 1 following an increase in NC enforceability, –1 following a decrease in NC enforceability, and 0 otherwise. I make this symmetric assumption for convenience, and because most of the changes that I use have a similar magnitude in the framework of the enforceability indexes constructed byStarr (2019)andBishara (2011). This approach is also consistent with that ofGarmaise (2011). I show inAppendix I.Athat results are robust to dropping the states in which NC enforceability decreases (Montana and South Carolina), as well as Georgia.

I allow the company fixed effectsγicand industry-year fixed effectsθjtcto vary by cohort, in order not to overweight repeated years in the fixed effect estimation. Industry is defined as four-digit NAICS code in all but the firm entry regressions, where I use LinkedIn’s industry categories in the absence of NAICS codes. Firm fixed effects absorb any time-invariant differences across firms, and absorb state fixed effects. Industry-year fixed effects absorb any time-varying differences across firms at the four-digit NAICS level.

In the main regressions, I do not include time-varying firm controls because such controls could be affected by the treatment (e.g., existing firms may grow larger as a result of the NC changes) and as a result cause inconsistent estimates of the treatment effect (Angrist and Pischke 2008). However, I show inAppendix I.Athat results are robust to including a panel of state- and firm-level controls. I double cluster standard errors at the state level and industry level to allow for covariance in outcomes within state and industry over time. I use four-digit NAICS for the industry clusters because using three- or two-digit NAICS generates smaller standard errors, and few clusters. All states enter the regressions (the untreated states enter as controls), so the number of clusters is sufficient.

Many of the predictions from Section 2 distinguish between knowledge firms and other firms, so I also employ a triple difference specification, interactingEquation (1)with an indicator for knowledge firm (or knowledge worker or sector). For example, to compare firm-level outcomes such as investment across knowledge and other firms, the specification is

α β β γ θ ϵ y ijstc = α + β ( treate d s * pos t t * K F i ) + β ( treate d s * pos t t ) + γ i c + θ kjtc + ϵ ikjstc . (2)

KFis an indicator, which takes the value of one if the firm employs a greater than median fraction of knowledge workers, and zero otherwise. The industry-year fixed effect is now estimated separately for knowledge firms (or knowledge workers or sectors as applicable), in addition to varying by cohort.

The main assumption underlying this empirical approach is that absent the NC enforcement changes, the average change in the treated and control groups would have been the same – the two groups would have continued to experience parallel trends. The coefficient estimate ontreated*post⁠, or the knowledge interaction oftreated*postwhere relevant, captures the additional change in the treated group, relative to the untreated group, following a change in NC enforcement.

InFigures 1,2, and3, I show that trends for the treated group changeafterthe NC enforceability change. For example, forFigure 2I estimate the following equation:

α β γ θ ϵ investment rate ijst = α + ∑ k β k ( treated s * k years to treatment ) + γ i + θ j t + ϵ ijst . (3)

Figure 2plots the estimates of the event study for knowledge firms: the coefficient estimate ontreated*kyearstotreatmentagainstkyearstotreatment⁠. The omitted time period is one year prior to the effective change in NC enforcement. Prior to the enforcement change, the estimated difference between the treatment and control groups is close to zero. However, following the change in enforcement, the investment rate in the treatment group increases significantly relative to the investment rate in the control group. The pattern actually mirrors, in the opposite direction, the relative decline in same-occupation departures inFigure 1.

InFigure A.1, I report the distribution of political and economic characteristics across states in my sample. Treated states appear in orange, while control states appear in blue. In general, treatment is fairly well distributed within these characteristics. Treated states have slightly higher population growth than untreated states, and they tend to be toward the middle of the political distribution. InAppendix I.A, I also show that trends in economic outcomes are very similar across treated and control states over the period of analysis. These similarities in economic conditions help reinforce the assumption that outcomes of interest in treated and control groups would have continued to evolve similarly in the absence of treatment.

For the purpose of enforcing NCs, the relevant jurisdiction is where the economic activity takes place, that is, where the worker is located (Lester and Ryan 2010). I gather information on employee location from LinkedIn and assign firms to the state in which they have the most employees.15Throughout my analysis, I show that results are stronger for more concentrated firms, for example where a minimum 40% of employees are located in the main state. This provides further evidence that the changes observed are linked to the treatment, which is less noisy for more concentrated firms.Appendix I.Agives the distribution of firms’ employee base concentration, and shows that the fraction of concentrated firms (at least 40% of employees in the main state) is very stable at around 80%.
article.5 Results:
This section reports the findings of my analysis. I first verify the impact of NCs on labor mobility, then turn to investment and entrepreneurship following the theoretical motivation from Section 2.

5.1 Labor mobility

Prior and concurrent work has established that NCs depress mobility across various groups (e.g. inventors [Marx, Strumsky, and Fleming 2009], executives [Garmaise 2011], physicians [Lavetti, Simon, and White 2019]). However, data limitations have made large-scale evidence of NC effects relatively scarce. Before turning to investment rate and firm entry, I establish the validity of my setting by showing an effect on the most direct outcome, in this case departure rates (Heath et al. 2023). To align with Section 2, I separate out the impact of NCs for knowledge workers relative to other workers.

Table 3reports the results. Each firm is split into knowledge and other workers, and thetreated*postterm is included both on its own and interacted with an indicator for the knowledge worker group. The regression specification is similar toEquation (2), but with an indicator for knowledge workers instead of knowledge firms as the interaction term.

Panel A shows the results for all firms, while panel B limits the sample to firms with a more concentrated employee footprint. Since NC enforceability largely depends on employee location, a firm with a more concentrated employee base will have a greater share of its employees affected by the change in NC enforceability. If the change in departure rates is due to the change in NC enforceability, we should expect the effect to be stronger in firms with a more concentrated employee base, as the treatment gets more precise.

The first column of panel A shows that following an increase in NC enforceability, the total departure rate drops for knowledge workers, but not for other workers. This is consistent with the higher incidence of NCs in knowledge occupations reported byStarr, Prescott, and Bishara (2021)and relisted inTable A.1. It also highlights the importance of separating out firms that depend on knowledge workers from others when turning to the investment results. In terms of magnitudes, the departure rate for knowledge workers declines by 86 basis points relative to other workers, and 47 basis points overall (⁠−0.861+0.396⁠). The total effect represents a decline of 7% relative to the sample mean departure rate of 6.9% for knowledge workers. In the more geographically concentrated sample of panel B, where a larger share of the firms’ employees are likely to be affected, this grows to a total decline of 11% relative to the sample mean. For the average firm with 672 knowledge workers, this represents a marginal 45 to 77 knowledge workers retained in a given year. For the median firm with 144 knowledge workers, the range is 10 to 16 retained workers.

The second and third columns separate out departures to the same occupation from departures to different occupations. Intuitively, within-occupation moves are more likely to capture competing moves, all else equal. The point estimates are not directly comparable across columns, since the numerators are restricted to different subsets while the denominator (number of employees) stays the same, but we can look at the magnitudes relative to the sample means reported inTable 2. In panel A, the estimates represent a total decline of 10% for within-occupation moves and 3% for moves to a different occupation for knowledge workers. In panel B, the estimates represent a total decline of 15% for within-occupation moves and 9% for moves to a different occupation for knowledge workers. These estimates are consistent with a larger effect for both within-occupation moves and for more geographically concentrated firms.

The fourth and fifth columns separate out departures within the same industry sector from departures to a different sector. Similar to the previous exercise, within-sector moves are more likely to capture competing moves, and that is where we expect NCs to have the most bite. In panel A, the estimates represent a total decline of 10% for within-sector moves and 4% for moves to a different sector for knowledge workers, relative to the sample means reported inTable 2. In panel B, the estimates represent a total decline of 16% for within-sector moves and 8% for moves to a different sector for knowledge workers.

Taken all together, the results show that mobility declines following increases in NC enforceability, specifically for knowledge workers. Movement is particularly affected where we would expect NCs to matter most: total departures, within-occupation and within-sector departures. As the treatment assignment becomes more precise (Panel B), the magnitude of the results becomes stronger. For the remainder of the analyses, I show results for both the baseline and the subset of companies with at least 40% of employees in the main state, and consistently find that results are not only robust but also larger for the latter set of firms.

To address concerns about potential unobserved changes happening in the same locations at the same time,Appendix I.Ashows that results still go through even after including state-year fixed effects. The results are then identified from the departure rate decrease of knowledge workers relative to other workers, within a given state and year. This specification absorbs state-specific economic conditions that could be changing over time. I also show that results are robust to dropping Georgia or South Carolina and Montana.

Finally, I consider whether there is a corresponding change in the number of workers joining firms (Table A.2). The results show a small slowdown in hiring among knowledge workers, offset to some extent by hiring other types of workers, and to a larger extent by retaining more knowledge workers. The magnitude of the effect is substantially smaller than for departures: relative to the sample mean, the total effect amounts to a 1% to 2% decline in hiring for knowledge workers, compared to a 7% to 11% decline in departure rates for the same group. This comes through in employment growth, which appears to increase for knowledge workers, though the effect is not statistically significant in the subsample of more geographically concentrated firms. Overall, the results are consistent with a slowdown in mobility for knowledge workers, which results in accumulation of human capital at incumbent firms.

5.2 Investment response

The results above show that NCs create meaningful frictions in labor mobility, specifically for workers in knowledge-intensive occupations. What effect do these frictions have on investment policies? To address this question, I use the framework from Section 2, starting with the baseline hypotheses for physical investment rate then turning to intangible investment. For further context, Appendix I.D provides an in-depth discussion of these results relative to the literature on EPLs, which cause a different type of labor friction that affects both physical and intangible investments.

5.2.1 Physical investment

Table 4presents results for the impact of stronger NC enforceability on firms’ physical investment rate. Since mobility declines specifically among knowledge workers, I split firms according to the share of their workforce that qualifies as knowledge workers. Panel A shows the results for all firms, while panel B splits out firms that have the greatest reliance on knowledge workers, that is, knowledge firms. In both panels, the first column reports results for the full sample, while the second column requires firms to be geographically concentrated in the state of assignment.

The results show that physical investment rate does not increase in a statistically significant way in the overall sample of firms, but it does increase in firms that rely on knowledge workers, that is, those most directly affected by the change in NC enforceability. The results are consistent with the labor-capital complementarity channel in H1 and the holdup channel in H2. The results also show that when it comes to physical capital, the firm-level effect dominates any decline in worker productivity, to net a total positive effect consistent with the second scenario in H3. The next section revisits this hypothesis for intangible capital.

In terms of magnitudes, the estimates in the first column of panel B indicate that knowledge firms increase physical investment by $18,000 for every million in assets relative to other firms. This goes up to $20,000 per million in assets for more geographically concentrated firms in the second column. In total, knowledge firms increase investment by $12,000 to $13,000 per million in assets, which represents 34% to 39% of the sample mean investment rate, and 26% to 29% of the standard deviation. To put this in an economic context, for the average knowledge firm with 1.7 billion in assets, the estimates indicate an increase of $20 million to $23 million in physical investment.16

InAppendix I.A, I show these results are robust to a number of alternative specifications. Including firm- and state-level controls or state-year fixed effects helps address concerns of concurrent firm- or state-wide economic trends, and results continue to hold in both cases. Across industry sectors (two-digit NAICS codes), knowledge firms consistently increase their physical investment rates when NCs become more enforceable, though estimates are not always statistically significant. The results are also robust to dropping Georgia, whose state change was enacted by legislature rather than court precedent, and South Carolina and Montana, in which NC enforceability decreased rather than increased. Lastly, I verify that all results are robust to using alternative definitions of the investment rate, with gross or net capital in the denominator rather than assets. I explore more cross-sectional predictions in Section 5.3.

5.2.2 Intangible investments

Section 2 outlined theoretical similarities as well as differences between physical and intangible investment policies. Although both forms of investment may in principle benefit from restricted labor mobility through the complementarity and holdup channels described above, intangible investments have also been singled out in both the theoretical and empirical literature as being particularly sensitive to employee incentives. In other settings, labor frictions have opposite effects on physical and intangible investments (Bai, Fairhurst, and Serfling 2020). What happens in the case of NC enforceability?

Table 5reports the results of regressions with two measures of intangible investments on the left hand side: R&D scaled by assets (columns 1 and 2), and the natural logarithm of one plus the number of patent applications (columns 3 and 4). Neither outcome changes in a statistically significant way following an increase in NC enforceability, either in the overall sample of firms (panel A) or in firms that rely on knowledge workers (panel B). The point estimates are also smaller relative to sample means, equivalent to between 8% and 10% of the sample mean for R&D and 1% of the sample mean for patent applications in knowledge firms.

Taken together, the results further the idea that intangible investments have different sensitivity to labor frictions than physical investment. 5.3 Cross-sectional heterogeneity and mechanisms

Several firm and industry characteristics may also affect the way that investment responds to labor mobility restrictions. This cross-sectional heterogeneity can further illuminate the mechanisms behind the investment response.

5.3.1 Labor-to-capital ratio and main business segment.

A few analyses can help us strengthen the tie between the investment response and the labor friction caused by NCs. One of these is looking at a firm’s labor-to-capital ratio to address the holdup problem of H2. Firms that spend relatively more on labor may be more sensitive to labor frictions, and in particular to holdup issues of employees over firms.

To evaluate whether this is the case, I split firms according to whether their labor-to-capital ratio is above or below median in the year before NC enforceability changes. This is possible in stacked regressions despite different treatment years, because each cohort in the stack surrounds a specific year of treatment. I create different “High L/K” indicators for each cohort, using the year before treatment specific to that cohort. This measure is independent of the knowledge firm indicator: 46% of knowledge firms are high labor-to-capital, very close to the 49% fraction in the full sample.17I then run regressions just like the one inEquation (2)andTable 4, panel B, but replacing the knowledge firm indicator with the indicator for high labor-to-capital ratio.

Table 6presents the results, with the full sample in the first column and the more geographically concentrated sample in the second column. The increase in the physical investment rate is driven by firms with a greater labor-to-capital ratio, consistent with these being more sensitive to holdup problems (H2). High L/K firms increase physical investment by $13,000 to $14,000 per million in assets relative to other firms, or $4,000 to $8,000 per million in assets relative to prior years. There is no statistically significant change in low L/K firms.

5.3.2 Competition.

One open question is the extent to which competition modulates the effect of NCs on investment.Kang and Fleming (2020)show that NCs reduce competition by slowing new firm entry and alleviating pressure on incumbents. Does this affect investment?

As described in Section 2, while NCs have a direct effect on knowledge firms through the impact on knowledge worker mobility, they may also have a spillover effect on other firms through NCs’ effect on competition. The benefit firms get from lower competition does not depend on their own workers being subject to NCs, or on labor-capital complementarity or the existence of holdup problems. What matters for this channel is the ex ante level of competition faced by firms. However, where competition was low, we would not expect this channel to matter as much (H4).

To explore the role of competition, I divide firms into terciles using price-cost margin in the year before NC enforceability changes.Table 8shows results for each tercile of competition. In panel A, the analysis includes all firms, while panel B restricts the sample to firms with 40% or more employees in the state of assignment. All regressions include industry-year fixed effects, to allow for within-industry comparisons.

I then interact all of the fixed effects with a cohort indicator. α β β γ θ ϵ ( I A ) itc = α + β 1 { treated i * post t * KF i } + β 2 { treated i * post t } + γ i c + θ kjtc + ϵ itc In panel A, the regressions use the full sample of matched firms. In panel B the sample of firms is limited to firms with at least 40% of employees in the main state. Standard errors in parentheses are double clustered at the state and industry levels. Industry is four-digit NAICS.

The results show that in the highest competition tercile, physical investment increases across all firms, at the rate of about $28,000 per million in assets. The investment rate also increases in knowledge firms, though by less: the total increase is about $5,000 per million in assets for knowledge firms in the high competition tercile (still a 16% increase relative to the sample mean for that group). One possible explanation for this pattern is that all firms in high-competition markets benefit from reduced competition but, within industry, knowledge firms disproportionately bear the enforcement and hiring costs associated with stricter NCs.18In the lowest competition tercile, the effect is reversed. Nonknowledge firms do not benefit from lower competition, and their investment rate actually decreases. In contrast, knowledge firms increase their investment rate significantly.

The results indicate that NCs do have spillover effects on nonknowledge firms via their effect on competition, consistent with H4. They also add nuance to the findings inTable 4, by showing that knowledge firms drive the results in low competition environments, but do not benefit as much in high competition environments.

5.4 Entrepreneurial activity and trade-offs

In this section I turn to the effect of NCs on new firm entry, both as a way of exploring the effect on competition through new entrants as well as understanding the investment decision for new firms. I cannot observe investment at new firms on the intensive margin (e.g., the investment rate within newly launched firms), but consider firm entry itself to be the main extensive margin outcome of new firm investment. Although this is a rough proxy, it helps to show the change in dynamics after NCs increase: while investment at established firms increases, as shown above, new firm entry declines, pointing to a trade-off in the effect of NCs on firms.

Existing work provides evidence that NCs hold individuals back from starting their own ventures (Hausman and Lavetti 2021;Starr, Balasubramanian, and Sakakibara 2018), but there is surprisingly little direct causal evidence that changes in NC enforceability decrease new firm entry in the aggregate, especially across a number of industries. As described in Section 2.3, the effect of NCs on firm entry depends on the net effect of multiple factors. Even if NCs impede the ability of some workers to leave and found new firms, if other workers (or new labor force participants) step up to replace them, new firm entry may not change. More generally, as stated in H6, if concerns about retention and holdup problems dominate for new firms, then increases in NC enforceability should increase firm entry. If instead issues of labor availability, incentives, or investment opportunities dominate for new firms, then increases in NC enforceability should decrease firm entry.

To investigate the impact of NCs on new firms, I collect information on firm entry using LinkedIn business pages, which include information on year founded, state, and a LinkedIn industry category. I convert this into a panel at the industry-state-year level, where my main outcome variable is the number of new firms started in each cell. To account for the fact that some states are much larger than others, I scale this outcome by state population, and include industry-state as well as industry-year fixed effects. Section 3.1.3 provides more detail on this data source and set-up.

Since the results so far show a differential effect for knowledge workers and incumbent knowledge firms, I split out LinkedIn industries that fall into the most closely related sectors: technology and professional, scientific & technical services. These sectors together contain 32 industries, and I refer to them collectively as the “knowledge sector.”

Table 9reports the effect of NCs on the number of new businesses founded per industry-year, first in the knowledge sector, then in other sectors, and lastly comparing the two in a triple difference specification. Panel A looks at all new firm entry, including solo firms (one person only, such as consulting). The estimates indicate that following higher NC enforceability, the entry rate per million people in the knowledge sector declines by 7% relative to the sample mean in that sector. In contrast, there is no statistically significant change in firm entry in other sectors (and the point estimate represents just 1% of the sample mean). Running these two results against each other in a triple difference specification confirms that the results are statistically significantly different.
