One concern with panel A may be that the numbers are driven by solo projects, such as consulting, which could be more susceptible to relabeling. In panel B, I exclude firms classified as “myself only” in LinkedIn. The results are similar, with estimates pointing to a decline of 9% relative to the sample mean in non-solo knowledge-sector firms. NCs do not have an impact on non-knowledge-sector firm entry.

A different concern may be that the numbers are actually driven by large company spin-offs, instead of the entry of truly new firms. In panel C, I limit firm entry to those firms with a size of 50 or fewer employees. The results are again very close to those in panel A, with estimates indicating a 6% decline in firm entry in knowledge sector firms, but effectively no change in non-knowledge-sector firms.

InAppendix I.A, I show that these results are robust to including state-year fixed effects and to dropping certain states. I also show some direct evidence of the labor availability issue by showing that knowledge workers’ departures to newly founded firms decline, although that decline is not statistically significant in the subset of “founder” departures. Overall, the results indicate that the potential benefits of increased NC protection are not enough to offset the costs for new firms looking to enter in knowledge sectors (H6).

Taken together with the results from Section 5.2, these findings illustrate a trade-off of restrictions on labor mobility, particularly for firms and new ventures that involve skilled workers. On the one hand, incumbent firms increase their physical investment rates when restrictions become more enforceable. On the other hand, greater enforceability of NCs corresponds to fewer new firms entering the economy.
article.6 Conclusion:
Recent research and policy proposals have renewed the debate over labor mobility restrictions. In particular, covenants not to compete (NCs) have received a lot of attention, mostly for their potentially negative effects on knowledge spillovers and entrepreneurship (Gilson 1999;Samila and Sorenson 2011). However, firms may view NC enforcement as a tool to safeguard capital investments.

In this paper, I examine how labor mobility frictions, such as NCs, affect investment decisions. I review the theoretical literature to generate a series of hypotheses for the conditions under which NCs should affect investment policy, yielding separate hypotheses for investment in physical capital, in intangibles, and in new firms. Another important distinction that emerges centers on workers’ occupation type. The relationship between physical and human capital differs across occupations, and firms more reliant on knowledge-intensive occupations are predicted to respond more strongly to NCs.

To test these hypotheses empirically, I identify a series of recent legal changes to the enforceability of NCs in various states, coming mostly from court rulings. I combine these with detailed data on employee movements from LinkedIn’s database of employment histories, deidentified and aggregated to the employer level. I show increases in NC enforceability substantially reduce the mobility of employees in knowledge-intensive occupations. The effect is particularly pronounced for moves to the same occupation, same sector, and when more of the firm’s employees are located in the treated state.

These changes in NC enforceability lead established firms to increase their investment rate in physical capital. This effect holds specifically for firms that heavily rely on knowledge-intensive occupations, have a higher potential for employee holdup problems, and face high ex ante competition. At the same time, investment in intangibles does not increase. This confirms the pattern documented in other contexts (Bai, Fairhurst, and Serfling 2020;Acharya, Baghai, and Subramanian 2014) that intangible and physical investment respond differently to labor frictions. Finally, the study demonstrates that stronger NCs result in fewer new firms entering the economy, specifically in knowledge sectors where existing firms increase investment.

These results point to an important trade-off of labor mobility restrictions, between promoting investment at existing firms on the one hand and dampening new firm entry on the other hand. A limitation of this study is that it cannot quantify the value of the marginal investment or of the new firms foregone, and a welfare analysis is beyond the scope of this paper. Nonetheless, this paper contributes to our understanding of the real effects of labor restrictions by showing how important NCs can be for investment decisions, both in terms of the physical investment rate at established firms and the decision to enter for new firms. Future research may build on this foundation to investigate additional consequences of NCs, and to refine our understanding of the trade-offs involved.
